original_reasoning: The Arabic gloss explicitly includes shortness, which matches a low seat or stool quite directly.
Arabic embedding into ground aligns well with Greek perfect 'they have held' through secure fixation.
حق is fixed emplacement or socket, and a spear-head is the inserted fixed head of a weapon.
Greek means 'you were announced', which matches the Arabic sense of appearing or becoming manifest.
Arabic leaning or slanting aligns well with Greek kneeling through the shared bodily core of bending downward.
The cluster looks like a preserved name or measure-word transmission; consonantal body and referential value stay close.
Arabic ساحة is an open court or space, and Greek λέσχη is a gathering place or hall; both preserve a place-for-assembly core.
The Arabic gloss explicitly gives something of earthenware, and Greek ἀλάβαστος is a vessel or mineral term; the durable container-material sense is plausibly preserved.
